BEARING 315
The Newsletter of Orienteering in the Pacific Northwest
March, 1995
[Image: Cascade Orienteering Club compass logo]

From The Tales Of Sacajawea

Elis Eberlein, SACO President

Is there a map in Walla Walla? After reading the club schedule, this is a relevant question as a meet is scheduled for May 20 in Walla Walla. The answer to the question is No, but work on the new map has begun. This map was initiated by new member, Rich McConchie. He uses the area around Bennington Lake, just east of Walla Walla, to teach ROTC classes. This is a popular recreation area for runners and hikers, among them Don Davis and his Dalmatian, Sadie. Don is the outdoor recreation writer in the local Walla Walla newspaper, and he was interested in featuring orienteering in a future column. The area is a project area for the US Army Corps of Engineers. Frank Skorina and I have started the fieldwork on this map which is going to be in the scale 1:10000. We have the advantage of using a really good base map, made by the Corps of Engineers from information in their GIS. They have the area very well surveyed. We could have gotten 2' contours, but we decided 10' would be sufficient. The base map does not have any vegetation features, but instead has exact numbered survey markers in the terrain and on the base map that can be used. This is very helpful.

The first event of the season is approaching. I strongly encourage all club members to bring a few friends to future meets. I thinks we are ready to try to expand the membership, and keep people going with local events on a regular basis.

See you all in the sage brush!

My First O' Meet in a Decade

Mark Van de Kamp

[Mark describes his experiences at the recent orienteering event at Drunken Charlie Lake. Additional comments about the event and results are at the back of this issue.]

Here is the long tale of my third orienteering meet ever (my first in almost ten years). During college I spent a semester in Wales (Trinity college in Carmarthen) and took a class in outdoor pursuits. As part of the class we "did" (is the term "ran in" used here?) two orienteering meets. I enjoyed them, so I had fond memories rekindled when I ran across this group on the net. When I saw a post advertising a meet near Seattle, I wrote down the contact number and decided to give the thing a try.

Several phone calls and a trip to the basement to dig out my old Suunto compass later, I found myself driving out into the country on a cold, drizzly Sunday morning. I guess it was only fitting that the directions to the meet (from the electronic O-hotline) were sufficient, but not very detailed. Perhaps the assumption is that vehicular navigation skills can be assumed.

The meet was at the end of a gated dirt road on land owned (I believe) by Weyerhaeuser or some other logging company [actually the Department of Natural Resources – Ed.], but fees were paid and maps obtained at the gate. I got out of my car and talked with the young fellow who was manning the cash box about which course I should attempt. There was a beginner, an intermediate, and a short, medium, and long advanced course. I'm in good physical shape, and I had my previous experience plus some backcountry bush-whacking experience, so I knew beginner was too easy. I was also a little worried that the intermediate might not present much of a challenge, but the word "advanced" gave me visions of being hopelessly lost in thickets of devil's club. I settled on the intermediate course and paid my five dollars. There were a few other orienteers around who all seemed to be debating between the medium or long advanced courses. When one of them asked why the fee was so much I was a little surprised. It was far less than I was used to paying for running races or, particularly, for triathlons.

At the end of the road I parked and wandered over to where a tent was set up. The older fellow who seemed to be in charge there explained that this was the finish area and that the start was off a ways up the road and in the woods. There weren't many people around,

Page 2

but they seemed to be congregating at a van parked a few feet away. When I asked what they were doing I was surprised to hear that they were copying the courses onto their maps. In Wales I had to start the course knowing only the first checkpoint, about a hundred yards down a clearly marked trail, before I got to see the rest of the course and copy it on my map. It seemed a little odd to be able to copy the course and then mill around planning my strategy without having the clock running. I should have asked which method was the normal procedure.

I set off for the start. I was a little worried about getting lost on my way to the start, since it was actually off in the woods a little bit, and my worries weren't helped any by the fact that the road I was walking on seemed to be marked as only a trail on the map. Apparently it had been graded after the map was made. I finally saw the sign ahead pointing into the woods and moved into the forest, following the green streamers that had been tied to tree branches. I found the start, where two women were just trading off their duties as starter, and took a moment to look at my map and collect my wits. The woman asked me what course I was doing and if I was new. She told me that if I got really, really lost, I could just go west and hit a busier dirt road with some traffic. I decided that I would just take this first experience back extra easy in order to avoid such problems.

My time came up, and I was off into the woods. I won't try to describe every control. I actually was never lost, and my planned paths from one control to the next always worked. A few legs were very easy, basically following a foot-path, but I was extra careful even on those legs, to stop and orient myself every 50 or 100 meters. It was a confidence builder to find each control and be reassured that my matching of the map to the features around me was actually correct. I didn't run very much, partly because the footing was pretty treacherous, with a light dusting of snow in many places, and partly because I was paranoid about running past my next mental checkpoint and getting lost. I ended up taking 72 minutes to find the 7 (or was it 8?, I can't remember) controls. I checked in at the tent. There was a box with a half-dozen or so donuts on the table, so I grabbed one and a glass of water. The older fellow I had talked to earlier asked to take a look at my map, so I showed it to him. I felt like a geek a couple of minutes later when a fellow in a wet running-suit and some kind of special gaiters came flying out of the woods to the finish. Not only had he finished the long advanced course in a time that made mine seem very-very pokey, he also asked the older fellow if he could have a donut and was told, "Why don't you just take ½. We don't have very many." I had just assumed that the donuts were the equivalent of the post-race goodies usually present in abundance at road-races.

Another person finished a few minutes later, also flying out of the woods and down the hill. It seems that, at least at this meet, there were not very many people just out to have a stimulating walk through the woods and that most people really push for speed. I'd have to get more confident about my ability to see the correspondence of the map to the features around me before I could go that fast. Other skills I think I would need would be an ability to keep a more detailed memory of the map in my head, and the confidence to really run across uneven terrain. I spent a lot of time mentally repeating things like "follow the stream until the sharp right bend", and then getting there and having to stand and look at the map, find my place, and plan my next little section. I also spent a lot of time carefully picking my way over logs and stepping cautiously into areas grown over by low shrubs and ground cover, wondering if I was going to turn my ankles or fall and impale myself on broken branches.

In general, I had a really good time wandering through the woods. I'm a little intrigued by the challenge of running more and using my conditioning, but I'm not sure I would want to make orienteering into as competitive an activity as I've found running and triathlons to be. It seems like orienteering has the space to allow someone like me to get a little bit away from competition and a little closer to just enjoying being out moving through the woods.

Volunteers Needed

Cascade O. C. desperately needs some new volunteers to help make things flow more smoothly. The executive board is not enough people with enough energy to put on meets, make new maps, etc. A few necessary positions are listed below:

__PH_0__ – one need not be an experienced orienteer for this one; just a love for talking on the phone is what it takes. Our membership coordinator has agreed to modify his computer data base to include people who are willing to help out at meets or otherwise and what they are willing to do if someone will do the phone work. This is a great opportunity for a new comer to the club to phone everyone on the roster not listed on the executive and chat. You can find out who has time to help in field-checking, or who has time to present short courses or work-shops on O' to the various schools and scout troops who inquire, or who has a new baby and is too busy to do anything for the next 2 years, or who else likes to make phone calls and can also be a member of the volunteer phone committee. This position could also be a jumping off point for writing a gossip column in "Bearing 315", the club newsletter, but it is certainly not required. Land-use permission and insurance coordinator -- each year the executive committee or a subset thereof produces a tentative schedule of O' events. It is necessary that someone get permission from the various land-owners (mostly government agencies) to use the area on the day that we want to and that restrooms be available for our members in exchange for the fees we are charged (public land use used to

Page 3

be free for O' around here when I started 14 years ago). This usually involves getting the land-owner named on the insurance policy for the meet that must be arranged with the United States Orienteering Federation (USOF) designated insurance carrier. So this involves tying letters and making phone calls and requires a more business-like manner than position 1 does. Again, one does not have to be an experienced orienteer for this.
__PH_0__ – we need someone with space in their garage or a separate shed that can be fitted with a combination lock so that meet directors can get the tables, chairs, signs, meet box, etc. and so that course designers can pick up the control markers and punches at times when a home's occupant might be at work. Debbie Newell has been letting us use her shed over 5 years but has acquired more stuff that she needs the shed for herself. Ideally, the location should be north of the ship canal but within the city limits, but – any offer will be welcomed. Again, no O' experience is necessary. Volunteers to field-check for new maps are highly desired and orienteering experience is a definite help.
* Course designers are always needed. Designing courses is a great way to improve your O' skills and for design you definitely need to have done intermediate level or higher courses at some of the more challenging maps such as ones upon which national meets have been held. Training is provided for those who are interested.

Anyone interested in any of these opportunities should phone the president or vice president (of Cascade O.C.) right away. Look on the list of officers in this newsletter for the phone numbers.

Field Notes

The United States Olympic Committee has named Eric Bone as Athlete of the Year for 1994 in the sport of Orienteering. Eric had a very successful year in 1994, winning the U.S. and North American Championships in the M20A category as well as having several fine performances at the Junior World Orienteering Championships in Poland and the men's elite World Cup events in Norway and Denmark. He received a certificate signed by Leroy T. Walker, President of the U.S. Olympic Committee, to mark this accomplishment. Eric is currently Training Coordinator for Cascade Orienteering Club and a founding member of the Husky Orienteering and Running Club. Congratulations, Eric! Orienteering on the Internet: Orienteering is now more prominent than ever on the Internet. A new Usenet newsgroup has just been created, "rec.sport.orienteering". For those of you without access to Usenet newsgroups, there is still the "O-Net", an worldwide mailing list dedicated to orienteering related discussions. To subscribe, send a message to "orienteering-request@graphics.cornell.edu" and specify if you want the digest or not. I have also compiled a local list of e-mail addresses called the "Husky O-Net" to which I send out information such as the daily training and race schedule, Husky club-related information and other items of local orienteering interest. To be included in this list, send a message to me (Bill Cusworth) at bill3@u.washington.edu. Orienteering related pages continue to be added to the World Wide Web at a high rate. For those of you with access, try the following URL's (addresses):
Orienteering and Rogaining Home Page: http://www2.aos.princeton.edu/rdslater/orienteering/ Husky Orienteering and Running Club Home Page: ftp://ftp.u.washington.edu/pub/user-supported/orienteering/HUSKinfo.html
Cascade Orienteering Club Home Page: http://halcyon.com/schuh/orienteering/coc.html A gray coat was found at the Point Defiance event. If it is yours, please call Mike Schuh at 783-3960.
Correction: Unlike previous years, EWOC will not be holding a orienteering event in association with the Bloomsday run. It was incorrectly listed in the 1995 tentative schedule. Correction: the new name of the annual St. Edward event was misreported in the last issue of Bearing 315. The actual name is "O' Lang Syne".
* We need more articles! As you can see, Bearing 315 needs more articles submitted by you, our loyal readers. If you want a newsletter that's more than just results and a schedule, please keep those articles coming!

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United States Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and the new Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

What equipment do I need?

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for rent at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

Event Fees

Event fees are charged on a per-registration basis: two people running together with the same map and punch card pay a single fee. Additional maps are available; the charge varies depending on the map. Fees vary, but the following are typical fees for local ("C") events with color maps. "A" and "B" meets, state championships, rogaines, and other events will cost somewhat more.

Rambo VII
Point Defiance Park
January 15, 1995
Nisqually Orienteers

The Rambo officials were hoping to use a new Pt. Defiance Park map but due to mitigating circumstances it did not happen. Instead, the course setter used the old map, the week between Christmas and New Years Day, and a few unmapped features to build this year's Rambo. The results proved to be a challenge to all comers. Out of the possible 3000 points two teams were one 100 point marker short of perfect scores. Rambo had only one hitch. Several, four, punches were snapped, popped or crackled off the controls. Most likely this was because there were many new orienteerers on the course. They punched and quickly left the control before their taped over punch card was free of the punch. The results shot the punch into the forest never to be seen again. I would like to say thank you to everyone who took the time to try Rambo. We will do it again next year.
Thank You
Steve Miner

Drunken Charlie Lake
March 5, 1995
Cascade Orienteering Club

Course Setter: Peter Golde
Land Permission: Peter Golde
Meet Director: Bill Cusworth
Assistant Meet Director: Eric Bone
Starts: Ellen Boomer, Don Atkinson
Finishes: Bruce McAlister, Bill Cusworth
Equipment: Bruce McAlister
Marker Pickup: Peter Golde, Don Atkinson, Bill Cusworth

The tone for this event was set when the person setting out the directional signs went down the wrong road! Drunken Charlie Lake just seems to do that to people. It routines sports the slowest per km times of any orienteering area in the Northwest, with its combination of downfall, steeps, slippery ground, lack of trails and other landmarks, and confusing topography. If you get lost here, you really know what lost is.

It was snowing lightly in the morning, which made the forest even more slippery and difficult to traverse than normal. The times reflect the difficult (perhaps overly difficult?) courses; everyone agreed that it was a real challenge. A few competitors didn't hang onto their punch cards quite hard enough, helping to contribute to the large number of DNFs.

On Course 3, Carl Moore proved that his years setting the Rambo course weren't wasted, edging out Don Denbo. On Course 4, John Rogers just blew away the competition with an impressive 70 minute performance. The elite competitors on course 5 ended up in a surprisingly close finish, with less then 7 minutes separating second from sixth place. Impressive performances by all!

Apologies to those people on Course 1 for a too difficult course, especially on the first control.

Winter O-Series: Final Results

This year's Winter O' Series resulted in some excellent competition. Although a misplaced marker caused the Priest Point Park meet results to be voided, and several other meets were questionable, everyone seemed to enjoy the series of events.
The men's competition was not decided until the final event. With Eric Bone (COC) tied with Paul Magnus (COC) but not competing because of course-setting duties, the race was between Magnus and Dave Enger (SAMM). Both Enger and Magnus needed a win to take the series outright, although Magnus could win the tiebreaker if Enger did not win the final day. After Magnus returned from the course with a less-than-perfect run, Enger had an excellent opportunity to win the day. When the dust had settled, locally-uknown Steve Cashen won the day, spoiling Enger's chance for victory by only 23 seconds.
In the women's division, two-time champion Debbie Newell (COC) made it three in a row, holding off a late-season challenge by Jana Urbanova (SAMM). Lisa Nordlund (CHUK) finished in third place.

What is USOF?

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including out-of-pockets costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!

Is there a map in Walla Walla? After reading the club schedule, this is a relevant question as a meet is scheduled for May 20 in Walla Walla. The answer to the question is No, but work on the new map has begun. This map was initiated by new member, Rich McConchie. He uses the area around Bennington Lake, just east of Walla Walla, to teach ROTC classes. This is a popular recreation area for runners and hikers, among them Don Davis and his Dalmatian, Sadie. Don is the outdoor recreation writer in the local Walla Walla newspaper, and he was interested in featuring orienteering in a future column. The area is a project area for the US Army Corps of Engineers. Frank Skorina and I have started the fieldwork on this map which is going to be in the scale 1:10000. We have the advantage of using a really good base map, made by the Corps of Engineers from information in their GIS. They have the area very well surveyed. We could have gotten 2' contours, but we decided 10' would be sufficient. The base map does not have any vegetation features, but instead has exact numbered survey markers in the terrain and on the base map that can be used. This is very helpful.

The first event of the season is approaching. I strongly encourage all club members to bring a few friends to future meets. I thinks we are ready to try to expand the membership, and keep people going with local events on a regular basis.
See you all in the sage brush!

My First O' Meet in a Decade
[Mark describes his experiences at the recent orienteering event at Drunken Charlie Lake. Additional comments about the event and results are at the back of this issue.]

Here is the long tale of my third orienteering meet ever (my first in almost ten years). During college I spent a semester in Wales (Trinity college in Carmarthen) and took a class in outdoor pursuits. As part of the class we "did" (is the term "ran in" used here?) two orienteering meets. I enjoyed them, so I had fond memories rekindled when I ran across this group on the net. When I saw a post advertising a meet near Seattle, I wrote down the contact number and decided to give the thing a try.

Several phone calls and a trip to the basement to dig out my old Suunto compass later, I found myself driving out into the country on a cold, drizzly Sunday morning. I guess it was only fitting that the directions to the meet (from the electronic O-hotline) were sufficient, but not very detailed. Perhaps the assumption is that vehicular navigation skills can be assumed.

The meet was at the end of a gated dirt road on land owned (I believe) by Weyerhaeuser or some other logging company [actually the Department of Natural Resources – Ed.], but fees were paid and maps obtained at the gate. I got out of my car and talked with the young fellow who was manning the cash box about which course I should attempt. There was a beginner, an intermediate, and a short, medium, and long advanced course. I'm in good physical shape, and I had my previous experience plus some backcountry bush-whacking experience, so I knew beginner was too easy. I was also a little worried that the intermediate might not present much of a challenge, but the word "advanced" gave me visions of being hopelessly lost in thickets of devil's club. I settled on the intermediate course and paid my five dollars. There were a few other orienteers around who all seemed to be debating between the medium or long advanced courses. When one of them asked why the fee was so much I was a little surprised. It was far less than I was used to paying for running races or, particularly, for triathlons.

At the end of the road I parked and wandered over to where a tent was set up. The older fellow who seemed to be in charge there explained that this was the finish area and that the start was off a ways up the road and in the woods. There weren't many people around,

but they seemed to be congregating at a van parked a few feet away. When I asked what they were doing I was surprised to hear that they were copying the courses onto their maps. In Wales I had to start the course knowing only the first checkpoint, about a hundred yards down a clearly marked trail, before I got to see the rest of the course and copy it on my map. It seemed a little odd to be able to copy the course and then mill around planning my strategy without having the clock running. I should have asked which method was the normal procedure.

I set off for the start. I was a little worried about getting lost on my way to the start, since it was actually off in the woods a little bit, and my worries weren't helped any by the fact that the road I was walking on seemed to be marked as only a trail on the map. Apparently it had been graded after the map was made. I finally saw the sign ahead pointing into the woods and moved into the forest, following the green streamers that had been tied to tree branches. I found the start, where two women were just trading off their duties as starter, and took a moment to look at my map and collect my wits. The woman asked me what course I was doing and if I was new. She told me that if I got really, really lost, I could just go west and hit a busier dirt road with some traffic. I decided that I would just take this first experience back extra easy in order to avoid such problems.

My time came up, and I was off into the woods. I won't try to describe every control. I actually was never lost, and my planned paths from one control to the next always worked. A few legs were very easy, basically following a foot-path, but I was extra careful even on those legs, to stop and orient myself every 50 or 100 meters. It was a confidence builder to find each control and be reassured that my matching of the map to the features around me was actually correct. I didn't run very much, partly because the footing was pretty treacherous, with a light dusting of snow in many places, and partly because I was paranoid about running past my next mental checkpoint and getting lost. I ended up taking 72 minutes to find the 7 (or was it 8?, I can't remember) controls. I checked in at the tent. There was a box with a half-dozen or so donuts on the table, so I grabbed one and a glass of water. The older fellow I had talked to earlier asked to take a look at my map, so I showed it to him. I felt like a geek a couple of minutes later when a fellow in a wet running-suit and some kind of special gaiters came flying out of the woods to the finish. Not only had he finished the long advanced course in a time that made mine seem very-very pokey, he also asked the older fellow if he could have a donut and was told, "Why don't you just take ½. We don't have very many." I had just assumed that the donuts were the equivalent of the post-race goodies usually present in abundance at road-races.

Another person finished a few minutes later, also flying out of the woods and down the hill. It seems that, at least at this meet, there were not very many people just out to have a stimulating walk through the woods and that most people really push for speed. I'd have to get more confident about my ability to see the correspondence of the map to the features around me before I could go that fast. Other skills I think I would need would be an ability to keep a more detailed memory of the map in my head, and the confidence to really run across uneven terrain. I spent a lot of time mentally repeating things like "follow the stream until the sharp right bend", and then getting there and having to stand and look at the map, find my place, and plan my next little section. I also spent a lot of time carefully picking my way over logs and stepping cautiously into areas grown over by low shrubs and ground cover, wondering if I was going to turn my ankles or fall and impale myself on broken branches.

In general, I had a really good time wandering through the woods. I'm a little intrigued by the challenge of running more and using my conditioning, but I'm not sure I would want to make orienteering into as competitive an activity as I've found running and triathlons to be. It seems like orienteering has the space to allow someone like me to get a little bit away from competition and a little closer to just enjoying being out moving through the woods.

Cascade O. C. desperately needs some new volunteers to help make things flow more smoothly. The executive board is not enough people with enough energy to put on meets, make new maps, etc. A few necessary positions are listed below:

Volunteer coordinator – one need not be an experienced orienteer for this one; just a love for talking on the phone is what it takes. Our membership coordinator has agreed to modify his computer data base to include people who are willing to help out at meets or otherwise and what they are willing to do if someone will do the phone work. This is a great opportunity for a new comer to the club to phone everyone on the roster not listed on the executive and chat. You can find out who has time to help in field-checking, or who has time to present short courses or work-shops on O' to the various schools and scout troops who inquire, or who has a new baby and is too busy to do anything for the next 2 years, or who else likes to make phone calls and can also be a member of the volunteer phone committee. This position could also be a jumping off point for writing a gossip column in "Bearing 315", the club newsletter, but it is certainly not required. Land-use permission and insurance coordinator -- each year the executive committee or a subset thereof produces a tentative schedule of O' events. It is necessary that someone get permission from the various land-owners (mostly government agencies) to use the area on the day that we want to and that restrooms be available for our members in exchange for the fees we are charged (public land use used to

be free for O' around here when I started 14 years ago). This usually involves getting the land-owner named on the insurance policy for the meet that must be arranged with the United States Orienteering Federation (USOF) designated insurance carrier. So this involves tying letters and making phone calls and requires a more business-like manner than position 1 does. Again, one does not have to be an experienced orienteer for this. Equipment shed volunteer – we need someone with space in their garage or a separate shed that can be fitted with a combination lock so that meet directors can get the tables, chairs, signs, meet box, etc. and so that course designers can pick up the control markers and punches at times when a home's occupant might be at work. Debbie Newell has been letting us use her shed over 5 years but has acquired more stuff that she needs the shed for herself. Ideally, the location should be north of the ship canal but within the city limits, but – any offer will be welcomed. Again, no O' experience is necessary. Volunteers to field-check for new maps are highly desired and orienteering experience is a definite help. Course designers are always needed. Designing courses is a great way to improve your O' skills and for design you definitely need to have done intermediate level or higher courses at some of the more challenging maps such as ones upon which national meets have been held. Training is provided for those who are interested.
Anyone interested in any of these opportunities should phone the president or vice president (of Cascade O.C.) right away. Look on the list of officers in this newsletter for the phone numbers.

The United States Olympic Committee has named Eric Bone as Athlete of the Year for 1994 in the sport of Orienteering. Eric had a very successful year in 1994, winning the U.S. and North American Championships in the M20A category as well as having several fine performances at the Junior World Orienteering Championships in Poland and the men's elite World Cup events in Norway and Denmark. He received a certificate signed by Leroy T. Walker, President of the U.S. Olympic Committee, to mark this accomplishment. Eric is currently Training Coordinator for Cascade Orienteering Club and a founding member of the Husky Orienteering and Running Club. Congratulations, Eric! Orienteering on the Internet: Orienteering is now more prominent than ever on the Internet. A new Usenet newsgroup has just been created, "rec.sport.orienteering". For those of you without access to Usenet newsgroups, there is still the "O-Net", an worldwide mailing list dedicated to orienteering related discussions. To subscribe, send a message to "orienteering-request@graphics.cornell.edu" and specify if you want the digest or not. I have also compiled a local list of e-mail addresses called the "Husky O-Net" to which I send out information such as the daily training and race schedule, Husky club-related information and other items of local orienteering interest. To be included in this list, send a message to me (Bill Cusworth) at bill3@u.washington.edu. Orienteering related pages continue to be added to the World Wide Web at a high rate. For those of you with access, try the following URL's (addresses): Orienteering and Rogaining Home Page: http://www2.aos.princeton.edu/rdslater/orienteering/ Husky Orienteering and Running Club Home Page: ftp://ftp.u.washington.edu/pub/user-supported/orienteering/HUSKinfo.html Cascade Orienteering Club Home Page: http://halcyon.com/schuh/orienteering/coc.html A gray coat was found at the Point Defiance event. If it is yours, please call Mike Schuh at 783-3960. Correction: Unlike previous years, EWOC will not be holding a orienteering event in association with the Bloomsday run. It was incorrectly listed in the 1995 tentative schedule. Correction: the new name of the annual St. Edward event was misreported in the last issue of Bearing 315. The actual name is "O' Lang Syne". We need more articles! As you can see, Bearing 315 needs more articles submitted by you, our loyal readers. If you want a newsletter that's more than just results and a schedule, please keep those articles coming!

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United States Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and the new Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for rent at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

The Rambo officials were hoping to use a new Pt. Defiance Park map but due to mitigating circumstances it did not happen. Instead, the course setter used the old map, the week between Christmas and New Years Day, and a few unmapped features to build this year's Rambo. The results proved to be a challenge to all comers. Out of the possible 3000 points two teams were one 100 point marker short of perfect scores. Rambo had only one hitch. Several, four, punches were snapped, popped or crackled off the controls. Most likely this was because there were many new orienteerers on the course. They punched and quickly left the control before their taped over punch card was free of the punch. The results shot the punch into the forest never to be seen again. I would like to say thank you to everyone who took the time to try Rambo. We will do it again next year. Thank You Steve Miner

The tone for this event was set when the person setting out the directional signs went down the wrong road! Drunken Charlie Lake just seems to do that to people. It routines sports the slowest per km times of any orienteering area in the Northwest, with its combination of downfall, steeps, slippery ground, lack of trails and other landmarks, and confusing topography. If you get lost here, you really know what lost is.

It was snowing lightly in the morning, which made the forest even more slippery and difficult to traverse than normal. The times reflect the difficult (perhaps overly difficult?) courses; everyone agreed that it was a real challenge. A few competitors didn't hang onto their punch cards quite hard enough, helping to contribute to the large number of DNFs.

On Course 3, Carl Moore proved that his years setting the Rambo course weren't wasted, edging out Don Denbo. On Course 4, John Rogers just blew away the competition with an impressive 70 minute performance. The elite competitors on course 5 ended up in a surprisingly close finish, with less then 7 minutes separating second from sixth place. Impressive performances by all!
Apologies to those people on Course 1 for a too difficult course, especially on the first control.

This year's Winter O' Series resulted in some excellent competition. Although a misplaced marker caused the Priest Point Park meet results to be voided, and several other meets were questionable, everyone seemed to enjoy the series of events. The men's competition was not decided until the final event. With Eric Bone (COC) tied with Paul Magnus (COC) but not competing because of course-setting duties, the race was between Magnus and Dave Enger (SAMM). Both Enger and Magnus needed a win to take the series outright, although Magnus could win the tiebreaker if Enger did not win the final day. After Magnus returned from the course with a less-than-perfect run, Enger had an excellent opportunity to win the day. When the dust had settled, locally-uknown Steve Cashen won the day, spoiling Enger's chance for victory by only 23 seconds. In the women's division, two-time champion Debbie Newell (COC) made it three in a row, holding off a late-season challenge by Jana Urbanova (SAMM). Lisa Nordlund (CHUK) finished in third place. Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including out-of-pockets costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!
